channel.title: Debt Desk
channel.description: Daily briefing with Morning Brief-style national news plus commercial real estate debt and multifamily market coverage. Deals, SOFR and Treasury curve context, CMBS, debt funds, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and HUD execution. Produced by Jeff Bechtel.
channel.itunes:author: Jeff Bechtel

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 11: Oil Risk, the China Setup, and a Narrow but Open Lending Market
item.description: Today's Monday Debt Desk starts with national coverage of the U.S.-Iran peace deadlock and renewed oil pressure, the coming Trump-Xi summit in Beijing, a labor market that still looks firmer than many expected, and a weak consumer-sentiment reading that keeps the inflation-growth mix uneasy. Then it turns to the May 8 Treasury curve and the latest official SOFR print available at run time, the rebound in commercial and multifamily lending volumes, fresh multifamily refinancings in Brooklyn and N
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Monday, May 11, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

We begin with the national picture, and this morning the biggest macro story is back to being energy, inflation risk, and foreign policy all at the same time. Reuters reported early Monday that oil jumped more than 3 percent after the United States and Iran failed to agree on a peace proposal drafted by Washington, with the Strait of Hormuz still largely closed and supply concerns back at the center of global markets. For borrowers and lenders, that matters immediately. When crude pushes back above the hundred-dollar line, the market does not just think about gasoline. It starts repricing inflation risk, transport costs, consumer pressure, and the possibility that long-end rates have a harder time moving lower.

That flows straight into trade and diplomacy. Reuters reported on May 10 that President Trump is scheduled to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing on May 14 and May 15, in the highest-profile U.S.-China meeting of the year so far. The goal is to stabilize a relationship that is still carrying stress from tariffs, Taiwan, and the broader geopolitical backdrop. Markets are not expecting a dramatic breakthrough, but they are looking for signs that the trade truce reached last fall can be extended rather than reopened. That matters because every debt market in this country is still trading a basic question: are we moving toward narrower, managed disputes, or back toward a more openly inflationary tariff regime?

Back at home, the labor market still looks more resilient than soft-landing skeptics expected. Reuters reported Friday that the U.S. economy added 115,000 jobs in April, above consensus, while the unemployment rate held at 4.3 percent. That is not a booming print, but it is strong enough to keep the Federal Reserve patient and strong enough to deny borrowers the cleaner rate-cut story many of them would prefer. For real estate credit, that means we are still in a world where capital has to work off today’s base rates, not an imminent rescue from sharply cheaper money.

The consumer side of the economy is sending a less comfortable signal. The University of Michigan’s preliminary May sentiment index, released Friday, fell to 48.2 from 49.8 in April, with consumers still citing higher prices and tariffs as major pressures. So the national setup this morning is a little conflicted, but in a very recognizable way. Payrolls are firm enough to keep recession calls in check. Consumers are uneasy enough to keep growth anxiety alive. Oil is back in the frame. And Washington is heading into a China meeting that could either cool one of the biggest inflation risks on the board or leave it hanging over the market.

That is the national setup. Now let’s move to the Debt Desk.

Start with rates, because this is still a market where every lending conversation eventually runs straight back to the curve. The latest official Treasury par curve available at run time is Friday, May 8. According to the U.S. Treasury, the 2-year closed at 3.90 percent, the 5-year at 4.02 percent, the 10-year at 4.38 percent, and the 30-year at 4.95 percent. The latest official SOFR print available at run time was for Thursday, May 7, and it was 3.60 percent, according to New York Fed data carried through FRED.

What that curve says is that financing got a little friendlier into the weekend, but not enough to change the underwriting regime. The 10-year below 4.4 percent is better than the hotter prints we saw earlier in the week. The 30-year holding just under 5 percent is also constructive relative to the most anxious inflation scenarios. But the front end is still telling you the Fed is not close to an easing campaign, and SOFR at 3.60 percent still leaves floating-rate borrowers with a meaningful all-in cost once lender spread, reserves, and structure get added on top.

That is why execution remains available, but selective. The best fresh macro evidence on that front came from the Mortgage Bankers Association on May 7. MBA said commercial and multifamily mortgage loan originations in the first quarter were up 52 percent from a year earlier. More specifically, depository lending jumped 80 percent year over year. That does not mean banks have turned loose and gone back to 2021 behavior. It does mean maturities are forcing activity and that banks are re-engaging where they already know the sponsorship, the asset, and the refinancing path.

That originations report also helps frame the tone across lender buckets. Banks look more willing to defend or refinance existing relationships than to stretch for new risk. Life companies remain disciplined but constructive for stabilized, lower-leverage product that can live with fixed-rate coupons. CMBS is open for the right collateral, but only when debt yield and sponsorship clear a very modern underwriting bar. And debt funds are still doing the heavy lifting on transition stories, construction gaps, and multifamily situations where flexibility matters more than cheapest possible execution.

The most useful spread context remains the latest broad lender read from CRED iQ. As of March 31, multifamily permanent loan spreads were about 154 basis points over the 10-year Treasury, industrial about 162, retail about 176, and office about 220. CRED iQ also pegged life-company 10-year quotes around 170 basis points at moderate leverage and CMBS conduit pricing near 250 basis points over the benchmark. That spread picture lines up with what the deals keep telling us. Multifamily is still getting the best treatment among major property types, office still prices as the outlier, and conduit remains available but not forgiving.

Fresh deal flow supports that read. Commercial Observer reported on May 8 that a joint venture between Ekstein Development Group and Standard Real Estate Investments secured a $58 million Barings refinancing for 1333 Broadway, a 106-unit mixed-income apartment building in Bushwick that opened last month. That is a clean example of lenders still showing up for new multifamily in dense markets when the asset quality is evident and the business plan is not heroic.

A different but equally important signal came from the fund side. Commercial Observer reported on May 7 that S3 Capital closed a multifamily-focused lending fund at $1.3 billion of investable capital, with expected origination capacity of about $4.3 billion. The detail that matters is not just the size. It is the stated use: first-lien construction lending for multifamily in supply-constrained markets. In other words, private credit is not just covering rescue capital and bridge debt. It is still raising real money to finance housing supply where regional banks pulled back.

So if you are trying to summarize the CRE debt market in one sentence this morning, it is this: the market is functioning, but it is rewarding clarity. The clearer the collateral, the sponsor, and the next financing step, the more lender lanes you have. The more the story depends on rate heroics or aggressive exits, the faster the market narrows.

Now shift specifically to multifamily, where the number of active capital channels still makes this the most financeable big property type, even with clear stress inside older loans.

The freshest HUD execution came from North Miami. Yield PRO reported on May 8 that Dwight Capital closed a $130 million HUD 223(f) refinance for The Gardens Residences, a 358-unit property in downtown North Miami. The proceeds retire a Dwight Mortgage Trust bridge loan, return equity to the borrower, and convert the asset into a 35-year fully amortizing fixed-rate structure. Dwight said it was the largest HUD loan ever closed in Florida and the largest multifamily loan approved by HUD’s Southeast region office. That matters because it shows the classic 2026 pattern: bridge debt is still a useful tool, but many borrowers ultimately want out of floating-rate exposure and into long-duration certainty once a project is seasoned enough.

Agency paper is still relevant too, even when it is not arriving through brand-new headline originations. Commercial Observer reported on May 6 that Bowery Properties bought Cascades at the Hammocks in Miami-Dade for $65.5 million and assumed the seller’s Freddie Mac-backed debt. That is important because loan assumptions keep acting as a practical transaction bridge when fresh-loan economics are less appealing. In a market like this, assumable agency debt is not just a footnote. It is often part of what gets a deal across the line.

Fannie Mae delivered a different kind of multifamily signal on May 6, announcing that certain multifamily MBS backed by loans originated under a credit facility are now eligible for resecuritization. That is capital-markets plumbing, not a borrower headline, but it still matters. When Fannie adds flexibility on the investor side, it helps support liquidity and keeps the agency machine more functional in a market that still values optionality.

HUD and FHA also made a process move that could matter more than it first appears. On May 4, HUD said it was streamlining environmental review requirements for multifamily applications by removing outdated provisions, restoring certain prior policies, and clarifying other standards inside the MAP guide. On its own, that does not cut coupons overnight. But if it reduces friction, cost, and timeline uncertainty for FHA-insured executions, it improves a lending lane that many apartment sponsors still view as one of the few places they can solve for long amortization and stability.

The one place where caution still needs to stay front and center is CMBS credit performance. Trepp’s April report, published May 4, showed the overall CMBS delinquency rate easing by one basis point to 7.54 percent, but multifamily delinquency rose 56 basis points to 7.71 percent. That split is the story. New lending is getting done, especially in better multifamily. But legacy apartment loans are still struggling in enough size to keep distress metrics elevated. So when people say apartments are financeable, that is true. When they say multifamily distress is gone, that is not true at all.

There is also an important distinction between types of apartment capital right now. Debt funds are increasingly the execution choice for newer product, lease-up stories, and construction or bridge situations. Banks are present, but more conservative and more relationship-driven. The agencies still matter tremendously, but much of their practical value is showing up through assumptions, secondary-market flexibility, and takeout potential. HUD remains the option for borrowers willing to trade speed for term, amortization, and certainty. None of those channels fully replaces the others. What makes multifamily workable is that all of them are still open, at least selectively.

Here is the concise markets snapshot this morning. The latest official Treasury curve available at run time was May 8 at 3.90 percent on the 2-year, 4.02 percent on the 5-year, 4.38 percent on the 10-year, and 4.95 percent on the 30-year. The latest official SOFR print available at run time was May 7 at 3.60 percent. Commercial and multifamily originations were up 52 percent year over year in the first quarter, with depository lending up 80 percent. Fresh apartment execution included a $58 million Barings refinance in Brooklyn, a $130 million HUD refinance in North Miami, and new private-credit dry powder from S3 Capital. At the same time, multifamily CMBS delinquency is still elevated at 7.71 percent, which is the reminder that today’s functioning market is still cleaning up yesterday’s loans.

One thing to watch next is whether this week’s inflation and consumer data reinforce the calmer tone in rates or knock it off balance. CPI lands Tuesday, retail sales on Thursday, and all of it now sits on top of a market that also has to think about oil and the Trump-Xi meeting later this week. If inflation stays contained and the China rhetoric cools, lenders may lean a little more constructive and more borrowers may choose to transact. If energy pressure spills back into inflation expectations, the long end can back up quickly and the market will go right back to rewarding only the cleanest stories.

The bottom line this morning is that the debt market is open, but still narrow in the way that matters. Nationally, the story is oil risk, trade diplomacy, resilient payrolls, and a consumer who still feels strained. In commercial real estate debt, that translates into a market where base rates are manageable but not cheap, where spreads have improved but not normalized, and where execution is best for borrowers who can show lenders exactly how the next chapter ends. Multifamily still has the deepest menu of financing options, from debt funds to agencies to HUD, but even there the dividing line is increasingly simple: clarity gets financed, and uncertainty pays up.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 7: Trade Diplomacy, a Steadier Curve, and Selective Apartment Liquidity
item.description: Today's Debt Desk starts with national coverage of tariff diplomacy around the Trump-Lula meeting, resilient jobless claims, John Roberts warning about the court's political image, Treasury's latest refunding signal, and a Reuters look at how immigration enforcement shifted federal law-enforcement resources in Minneapolis. Then it turns to the May 6 Treasury curve and SOFR, lender tone across banks, life companies, CMBS, and private credit, fresh multifamily refinancings in Denver and White Plai
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Thursday, May 7, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

We begin with the national picture before we turn to commercial real estate debt and multifamily capital.

The first national story this morning is trade diplomacy, because it is landing right at the intersection of inflation, industrial planning, and rate psychology. Reuters reported early Thursday that Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva is at the White House seeking to head off new U.S. tariffs and reopen a more workable conversation with President Trump on trade, critical minerals, and organized crime. On its own, a Brazil meeting would be notable but not market-defining. What makes it matter more is the backdrop. The White House is juggling multiple tariff fronts at once, and investors are also looking ahead to Trump’s scheduled trip to Beijing next week for a summit with Xi Jinping. For markets, the key question is whether Washington is moving toward narrower, negotiated trade fights or toward a wider cost-push regime that keeps pressure on long-end yields.

The second story is the labor market, which continues to refuse to crack in a dramatic way. Reuters reported Thursday morning that initial jobless claims rose to 200,000 for the week ended May 2, a little firmer than the prior week but still below expectations and still consistent with low layoffs. That matters because it keeps the basic macro story intact. Growth may be uneven, and the market still has plenty to worry about on inflation, oil, and tariffs, but there is not yet a clean labor-market deterioration that would force the Fed back toward an easier stance. In other words, borrowers hoping for a fast, rate-cut-driven improvement in financing conditions still do not have that catalyst in hand.

The third story is institutional credibility, and this one came from the Supreme Court itself. Reuters reported Wednesday night that Chief Justice John Roberts said he is concerned by the public perception that the court is acting like a political institution rather than applying the law. In a typical market environment that might feel abstract. Right now it does not. Courts are shaping operating assumptions across elections, healthcare, regulation, and executive power, and when the chief justice is openly acknowledging a legitimacy problem, it reinforces the idea that policy uncertainty is still broad rather than isolated. Markets do not just price statutes and Fed meetings. They also price confidence in institutions that settle disputes.

The fourth story is Treasury financing. The Treasury Department said Wednesday that it will maintain coupon and floating-rate note auction sizes for at least the next several quarters, while laying out a $125 billion refunding package that raises about $41.7 billion in new cash from private investors. For the broader market, that was a signal of caution and continuity. Treasury is not trying to shock the market with larger long-end supply right after a rough stretch in nominal yields and inflation expectations. That does not remove the fiscal overhang, but it does tell investors that Washington is trying to avoid making the bond market’s job harder in the near term.

The fifth story is domestic enforcement and the strain it can put on federal capacity. Reuters reported Thursday that the administration’s immigration operation in Minneapolis sharply slowed other federal criminal investigations there as personnel were diverted into the crackdown. That is not a direct debt-market story, but it is another reminder that policy choices are carrying secondary effects well beyond their stated target.

That is the national setup this morning. Now let us move to the Debt Desk.

Start with rates, because the market has been trading headline by headline and the only way to stay grounded is to anchor to the latest verified prints. The most recent official Treasury par curve available at run time is Wednesday, May 6. The 2-year closed at 3.87 percent, the 5-year at 3.99 percent, the 10-year at 4.36 percent, and the 30-year at 4.94 percent, according to the U.S. Treasury’s daily par yield curve table. The latest SOFR print is also for May 6, and it came in at 3.61 percent, updated by the New York Fed through the FRED series Thursday morning.

That curve is off the hotter levels seen earlier in the week, but it is still not cheap financing. The 2-year below 3.9 percent says the front end is not pricing immediate additional tightening, but it also is not pricing an urgent need to ease. The 5-year just under 4 percent still matters a lot for commercial real estate because that is where much of the market effectively lives when lenders and borrowers debate shorter duration, extensions, and bridge-to-perm strategies. The 10-year at 4.36 percent is workable, but only for deals that can survive sober leverage and debt-yield tests. And the 30-year still sitting just under 5 percent is the market’s way of saying long-duration capital still demands compensation for inflation, fiscal supply, and policy noise.

Treasury’s refunding message reinforces that tone. The government is not increasing nominal coupon sizes right now, which helps avoid fresh pressure on the long end, but it is still leaning on bills and watching borrowing needs carefully.

SOFR at 3.61 percent tells a similar story for floating-rate borrowers. The pain level is meaningfully better than it was at the tightest point of the cycle, but once lender spread, floor structure, and reserve requirements get layered on top, bridge debt is still expensive enough that it has to buy time for a real business-plan outcome. A floating-rate refinance only makes sense when there is a believable path to lease-up, seasoning, partial paydown, or an eventual agency or life-company takeout.

That is exactly what the latest deal flow is showing. In Denver, Commercial Observer reported on May 5 that a BMC Investments and Rockpoint venture secured a $68.3 million refinancing from Prime Finance on Madison at Copperleaf, a 330-unit multifamily community in Aurora. Prime provided a three-year floating-rate bridge loan. That is a very recognizable 2026 pattern. Private credit is still there for newer apartment product, but it is showing up as transition capital, not charity. If a sponsor can explain the next two or three years clearly, there is money.

A larger version of the same story printed in White Plains. Commercial Observer also reported May 5 that RXR and Korman Communities secured $269 million to refinance Hamilton Green, a 477-unit recently completed multifamily development, from a lending group led by Blackstone Real Estate Debt Strategies and Canyon Partners Real Estate. The project is already nearly fully leased, which matters. Private credit will still write large checks, but the cleanest executions are going to assets that have already done most of the operational proving.

Taken together, those two transactions say a lot about execution tone. Banks are still active, but the freshest apartment headlines are skewing toward private credit and bridge lenders willing to underwrite a transition period. That suggests the market still wants flexibility more than pure maximum proceeds.

For banks, the read-through is that they remain selective and relationship-oriented. They will still show up for straightforward collateral, stronger sponsorship, and construction or stabilization plans they understand. But when a deal needs more duration flexibility, more lease-up patience, or more tolerance for market noise, private credit is still doing a lot of the heavy lifting.

Life companies remain important too, even if they are not dominating the freshest headlines this week. The absence of splashy life-company headlines is itself a signal. Their best lane is still the same one it has been for a while: high-quality, stabilized multifamily and industrial where certainty, fixed rate structure, and moderate leverage matter more than stretching proceeds. They are not the rescue capital of this cycle. They are the clean-execution capital.

CMBS remains open, but the gap between front-book execution and back-book pain is still central. The latest available Trepp delinquency report, published May 4, showed the overall CMBS delinquency rate slipping by one basis point to 7.54 percent in April. That sounds mildly constructive until you get to the apartment line. Multifamily delinquency rose 56 basis points to 7.71 percent. That is the split the market keeps living with. Fresh issuance can function, especially for stronger collateral, while older multifamily and office loans continue to strain under a rate structure they were not built for.

That distinction matters for spreads. When people say CMBS is open, they are generally talking about new transactions that can withstand current underwriting and current debt costs. When owners complain that debt still feels tight, they are usually talking about legacy loans and refinancing math that still does not pencil cleanly.

Now move specifically to multifamily, where the market still has the most financing lanes, even if every lane comes with conditions.

One fresh agency read came out of Miami on May 6. Commercial Observer reported that Bowery Properties bought Cascades at the Hammocks for $65.5 million and assumed the seller’s Freddie Mac-backed loan, which carries about $40.6 million of outstanding debt. That is not a blockbuster new origination, but it is useful evidence that existing agency paper still matters in transactions and can help assets move when assumption economics are better than starting from scratch in today’s market.

A second fresh agency-market signal came from Fannie Mae itself. On May 6, Fannie announced that certain multifamily mortgage-backed securities backed by loans originated under a credit facility are now eligible for resecuritization. That is more of a capital-markets plumbing story than a borrower headline, but it still matters. It is a reminder that the agency ecosystem is trying to add flexibility and support liquidity for multifamily investors even while the broader market remains selective.

On the HUD and FHA side, Yield PRO reported on May 5 that Berkadia’s FHA and HUD team recently closed more than $115 million across five HUD 221(d)(4) and 223(f) multifamily loans in Virginia, including both construction-perm and refinance executions. HUD remains one of the few channels that can solve for term, amortization, and stability when sponsors can tolerate the process and fit the program.

That is why multifamily still looks more financeable than most other major property types even as distress persists in older securitized loans. Apartments still have multiple working outlets. Borrowers can move between debt funds, banks, agency, and HUD depending on asset age, affordability mix, stabilization status, and leverage needs. Office simply does not have that same menu.

One subtle but important point here is that today’s apartment market is not dividing itself cleanly between good assets and bad assets. It is dividing itself between stories that can be underwritten with a visible next step and stories that still depend on heroic assumptions about future rates or exit values.

Here is the concise markets snapshot this morning. The latest official Treasury curve is May 6 at 3.87 percent on the 2-year, 3.99 percent on the 5-year, 4.36 percent on the 10-year, and 4.94 percent on the 30-year. SOFR printed 3.61 percent for May 6. Treasury is holding coupon and floating-rate note auction sizes steady for at least the next several quarters, which should help keep the immediate supply backdrop from getting worse. In CRE credit, private lenders are still closing multifamily transition deals, agency paper is still showing up in transactions and market-structure updates, and CMBS remains functional on the new side even as multifamily delinquency in the legacy book stays elevated.

One thing to watch next is whether calmer rates can actually broaden execution beyond the cleanest apartment stories. If the curve holds around these levels and oil keeps backing off, more borrowers may decide the market is finally stable enough to refinance or recapitalize. But if macro headlines reheat and the long end backs up again, lenders will keep reserving their best execution for assets that are already mostly de-risked. That would leave private credit doing even more of the transition work while banks, agencies, and life companies stay disciplined.

The bottom line this morning is that the market is open, but still narrow where it matters. Nationally, the tone is being set by trade diplomacy, resilient labor data, institutional strain, and a Treasury market that is still searching for balance. In real estate debt, that translates into a financing environment where base rates are better than the worst prints of the week, but still high enough to punish weak assumptions. Multifamily remains the most workable large-property lending lane, with debt funds refinancing newer product, agency paper still active in transactions, and HUD still absorbing deals that need long-duration certainty.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 5: Oil Risk, Court Volatility, and a Still-Open Apartment Debt Window
item.description: Today's Debt Desk opens with national coverage of the Strait of Hormuz shipping threat, the latest Supreme Court mifepristone ruling, and the next trade escalation with Europe. Then it turns to the May 4 Treasury curve, SOFR direction, CMBS and multifamily delinquency trends, lender tone across banks, life companies, CMBS and debt funds, fresh apartment financings from Florida, New Jersey and Charlotte, a concise markets snapshot, and one thing to watch.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Tuesday, May 5, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

We begin with the national picture before we turn to commercial real estate debt and multifamily capital.

The first story this morning is still the one with the fastest path into rates, fuel costs, and lender psychology, and that is the renewed shipping stress around the Strait of Hormuz. Reuters reported Monday that the United States began helping ships stranded in the waterway, while a tanker in the region was also reported hit by projectiles. Markets do not need a full supply shock for this to matter. They only need enough disruption to keep oil traders, inflation watchers, and bond desks on edge. For commercial real estate, that matters because every new energy headline makes it harder to argue for an easy move lower in long-term borrowing costs. When the market is already trying to price sticky inflation, a live geopolitical risk in one of the most important shipping lanes in the world keeps the rate floor feeling real.

The second national story is the Supreme Court moving quickly on mifepristone. Reuters reported late Monday that the court restored mail-order access to the abortion pill while the legal fight continues, reversing a lower-court restriction for now. That does not directly change CMBS spreads this morning, but it does reinforce something broader that borrowers and lenders are already living with. Policy volatility is not confined to Congress or the White House. It is also coming through emergency court actions that can alter operating assumptions quickly in healthcare, employment, and regulated industries. For markets, that is another reason volatility remains a feature rather than an exception.

The third story is trade. The administration's threat to raise tariffs on European autos to 25 percent next week is still hanging over the market, and it matters well beyond the car business. A tariff shock of that size would hit supply chains, input costs, and business planning at the same time the Treasury market is already coping with higher oil risk and a heavier fiscal backdrop. For lenders, the issue is not whether a single tariff headline kills lending appetite. It is whether repeated tariff headlines make corporate tenants, consumers, and investors a little less willing to make clean forward assumptions. In today's market, that uncertainty tends to widen underwriting discipline even if quoted spreads do not immediately jump.

That is the national setup this morning. Now let us move to the Debt Desk.

Start with rates, because nothing in this market makes sense without the base-rate backdrop. The latest official Treasury par curve available at run time is Monday, May 4. The 2-year closed at 3.95 percent, the 5-year at 4.08 percent, the 10-year at 4.45 percent, and the 30-year at 5.02 percent. That is a meaningful backup from Friday's already-elevated levels, and it matters across the curve, not just at the benchmark 10-year.

The 2-year at 3.95 percent says the front end still does not believe a fast rescue is coming. The 5-year at 4.08 percent matters because a lot of real loan sizing effectively lives there even when borrowers talk about the 10-year. The 10-year at 4.45 percent is not a shut market, but it is still expensive enough that permanent debt only works cleanly when leverage is disciplined and the story is credible. And the 30-year back above 5 percent is the market's way of saying inflation risk, supply risk, and fiscal risk still need to be paid for. In other words, this is not a market where a modest spread tightening suddenly fixes proceeds.

SOFR is telling a similar story. The latest New York Fed publication still leaves overnight SOFR in the mid-3.6 percent range, so floating-rate debt is far less painful than it was at the peak of the tightening cycle but still not cheap once a lender's spread is layered on top. For bridge borrowers, that means time-buying refinancings can work, but only if there is an actual path to lease-up, burn-off, or recapitalization. For debt funds, it means there is still a real business in providing flexibility, but that flexibility continues to be priced, not given away.

That rates backdrop is exactly why the tone across lender groups remains constructive but selective. Banks are still open for relationship business, especially when there are deposits, modest leverage, and straightforward collateral. Life companies remain one of the best executions for stabilized multifamily and industrial where sponsors can trade max proceeds for certainty and a cleaner spread discussion. CMBS is open for assets that can survive realistic underwriting, but the market is not giving anyone a free pass on structure. Debt funds are still the most flexible part of the stack for business-plan assets, construction, and recapitalizations, but they are charging for that flexibility because the macro backdrop still warrants it.

There were fresh reminders in the last twenty-four hours that deals are still getting done, especially when the story is clear. In South Florida, Omega Funds closed what Multihousing News described as the largest HUD loan ever in the state, a 241 million dollar refinancing for Gardens Residences in North Miami. That is a very specific signal. HUD remains one of the few channels where a borrower can still solve meaningfully for duration and proceeds if the asset is stabilized enough and the sponsor can live with process.

We also got another read on floating-rate execution. Multihousing News reported Monday that Skylight Real Estate Partners and PCCP secured a 110.1 million dollar loan from MF1 Capital to refinance The Wyldes, a 399-unit apartment community in Englewood, New Jersey. That loan replaced construction debt and gives the sponsors more runway as the asset seasons. The takeaway is not that bridge money is suddenly loose. The takeaway is that well-leased multifamily can still refinance into floating-rate debt if there is a believable next step and a capital partner willing to underwrite that transition.

On the construction side, Charlotte stayed active. The Fallon Company just landed 101 million dollars to build The Henry at Camp North End, a 370-unit mixed-income project, with a financing package anchored by TD Bank construction debt and RXR as equity partner. That deal matters because it shows bank construction capital has not disappeared. It has simply narrowed. If a sponsor brings strong institutional backing, an understandable business plan, and a project in a market lenders still want exposure to, bank-led construction financing can still clear.

If you put those three deals together, you get a clean map of the market. HUD is open for the right stabilized product. Banks are still lending into construction when the capital stack is credible. Debt funds remain willing to refinance newer multifamily into floating-rate structures when the transition risk is understandable. The market still has capital. It just does not have much tolerance for ambiguity.

Now turn to CMBS, because the securitized market is still sending a split message. Trepp's fresh April delinquency report showed overall CMBS delinquency ticking down by a basis point to 7.54 percent, which on the surface looks slightly better. But beneath that headline, multifamily delinquency rose another 30 basis points to 7.71 percent, which is the highest level since the aftermath of the financial crisis. That is an important distinction. The front end of the market can still execute. New deals can still price. But legacy apartment loans written into lower-rate assumptions are still working through a much tougher refinance environment.

That split matters for spread talk. When people say CMBS is open, they are mostly talking about the front book, where cleaner assets and stronger sponsorship can still find buyers. When borrowers say the market feels tighter than the quoted spread sheets suggest, they are usually talking about the back book, where maturity defaults, cash flow misses, and old leverage assumptions are colliding with today's rate structure. Both views are true. The mistake is thinking a functioning new-issue market means legacy stress has been solved.

Multifamily remains the broadest live lending lane, but even there the message is not easy money. It is multiple execution lanes with conditions. HUD is still a powerful option for durable, stabilized assets. Agency debt remains the cleanest standard execution for borrowers who can satisfy underwriting and affordability requirements. CMBS can work when the asset fits securitization and structure is acceptable. Debt funds still own the transitional lane. The reason apartment owners are still getting attention is not that the sector is painless. It is that compared with office or shakier retail stories, apartments still offer more lenders a path to conviction.

That also helps explain why agency and government-backed channels remain central even when we do not get a fresh volume headline every morning. The market keeps coming back to those lanes because they provide something the private market often does not at moments like this, and that is durable execution. When Treasury yields are this high and term premiums are still climbing, borrowers care less about theoretical best pricing and more about certainty, proceeds, and the ability to close.

For life companies, the environment is still favorable in relative terms, but only for a narrow band of product. Stabilized multifamily and industrial with strong sponsorship remain the cleanest fits. That is not where the bulk of distress sits, so life-company money continues to matter more as a best-in-class execution lane than as a systemwide cure. Banks are similar. They can still be sharp on relationship loans, including selected construction transactions like Charlotte, but they are not stepping out indiscriminately.

Debt funds, meanwhile, are still acting as the shock absorbers of the market. They are taking on lease-up risk, transition risk, and recapitalization risk that many banks and life companies do not want. But because the rate environment still leaves little room for error, debt-fund capital is demanding strong covenants, good business plans, and compensation for uncertainty. That is why the MF1 refinancing in New Jersey matters. It is not just a deal headline. It is evidence that private credit is still open when the risk can be explained in a way that matches today's base rates.

Here is the concise markets snapshot this morning. The official Treasury curve backed up on May 4 to 3.95 percent on the 2-year, 4.08 percent on the 5-year, 4.45 percent on the 10-year, and 5.02 percent on the 30-year. SOFR remains in the mid-3.6 percent range, so floating-rate debt is workable but still expensive in absolute terms. CMBS headline delinquency improved marginally in April, but multifamily delinquency moved higher again to 7.71 percent, underscoring that old apartment loans remain under pressure. Execution is still available across HUD, banks, agency, CMBS, and debt funds, but the market is paying up for complexity and rewarding clean stories.

One thing to watch now is whether the macro backup in rates starts to harden lender behavior just as more borrowers come back after month-end. If oil stays elevated, if the tariff story with Europe escalates, or if the Treasury curve keeps moving higher out the long end, lenders have every reason to stay conservative on leverage and debt yield. But if rates can hold here and multifamily deal flow keeps printing across HUD, banks, and private credit, apartments should remain the most financeable large property type in the market even with distress still lingering in older CMBS loans.

The bottom line this morning is that the market remains open, but selective and expensive. Nationally, the tone is being set by live shipping and oil risk, a fast-moving Supreme Court docket, and the next tariff fight with Europe. In debt markets, that translates into available capital with very little slack. The good news is that multifamily still has real outlets, from a record Florida HUD refinance to a New Jersey debt-fund refi to a bank-backed mixed-income construction loan in Charlotte. The harder truth is that elevated base rates are still doing most of the work. In this market, the winners are still the same: strong sponsorship, realistic leverage, and asset stories a lender can underwrite without needing a heroic call on rates.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 4: Hormuz Shipping Risk, Fresh Legal Fights, and a Selective Multifamily Credit Window
item.description: Today's Debt Desk opens with national coverage of the U.S. move to help ships stranded in the Strait of Hormuz, the emergency Supreme Court fight over mail-order mifepristone access, the House blueprint for more immigration-enforcement funding, and the Trump administration's new threat to raise tariffs on European autos. Then it turns to the latest verified Treasury curve, SOFR direction, CMBS spreads and delinquency, lender tone across banks, life companies, CMBS, and debt funds, fresh multifam
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Monday, May 4, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

We begin with the national picture before we turn to commercial real estate debt and multifamily capital.

The biggest macro story this morning is still energy, shipping, and the inflation risk that comes with both. Overnight, Reuters reported that a tanker was hit by unknown projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz just as President Trump said the United States would begin helping free ships stranded in the waterway starting Monday. The administration is framing the move as a humanitarian effort for neutral countries, but for markets the real takeaway is that supply-chain stress in one of the most important energy corridors in the world is still very much alive. Even with crude pulling back from last week's highs, prices are still elevated enough to keep inflation worries in the conversation. For rates markets, that matters because it makes it harder to price an easy, quick move lower in yields when oil can jump again on any escalation headline.

The second national story is the abortion-pill fight moving rapidly back toward the Supreme Court. Reuters reported late Sunday that Danco Laboratories and GenBioPro asked the high court to restore mail-order access to mifepristone after the Fifth Circuit temporarily blocked deliveries. That appeal came only a day after the lower court ruling, which means this is no longer a slow-moving social issue sitting in the background. It is an immediate legal and political story with nationwide implications. The practical point for markets is not that abortion litigation directly moves bond spreads this morning. It is that the Supreme Court remains an active source of policy volatility, and this is another reminder that operating rules in healthcare, labor, and state-level regulation can change fast when a case accelerates onto the emergency docket.

The third story is in Washington, where the House has now advanced the framework for more immigration-enforcement funding. Reuters reported on April 29 that the House approved a three-year budget plan that could pave the way for roughly 70 billion dollars in additional immigration-enforcement spending after a five-hour vote hold to secure enough Republican support. This is still a blueprint rather than final enacted funding, but it matters because it shows that the immigration fight is moving from campaign rhetoric back into the appropriations and reconciliation machinery. That keeps the fiscal conversation noisy at the same time the Treasury market is already digesting higher oil, persistent inflation pressure, and an election-year policy mix that still leans toward more volatility rather than less.

The fourth story is trade. On May 1, President Trump said the United States would raise tariffs on cars and trucks from the European Union to 25 percent next week, arguing that the bloc was not complying with last year's trade understanding. That matters beyond the auto industry. It is another example of how quickly trade policy can jump from legal cleanup after the Supreme Court's tariff decision into a new round of sector-specific action. For corporate borrowers and lenders, tariff headlines are not abstract anymore. They influence capex decisions, inventory planning, consumer-price assumptions, and earnings visibility. In a market already dealing with expensive base rates, another trade flare-up only adds to the case for cautious underwriting.

That is the national setup this morning. Now let us move to the Debt Desk.

Start with rates, because every financing conversation still starts there. The latest official Treasury par curve available this morning is Friday, May 1. The 2-year closed at 3.88 percent, the 5-year at 4.02 percent, the 10-year at 4.39 percent, and the 30-year at 4.97 percent. That is not just a high 10-year story. The front end is still firm enough to keep floating-rate carry meaningful. The belly of the curve is still expensive enough to squeeze refinance math. And the long end remains high enough that permanent debt proceeds do not suddenly open up just because credit spreads behave a little better than they did a year ago.

The 2-year at 3.88 percent tells you the market still does not believe a near-term rescue is on the way. The 5-year at 4.02 percent matters because a lot of real-world loan sizing effectively lives in that part of the curve even when borrowers fixate on the 10-year headline. The 10-year at 4.39 percent is workable for clean permanent executions, but only with disciplined leverage and believable cash flow. And the 30-year sitting just under 5 percent tells you long-duration money still wants to be paid for inflation risk, fiscal uncertainty, and supply concerns. Put differently, the curve has stabilized, but it has stabilized in a place that still forces discipline.

SOFR is telling the same story. The latest official New York Fed publication still leaves overnight SOFR in the mid-3.6 percent range, so floating-rate debt is no longer at the panic levels borrowers dealt with during the peak tightening phase, but it is nowhere near cheap once a real spread gets layered on top. For bridge lenders, that means business plans still need actual momentum. For borrowers, it means a refinance into floating-rate paper can buy time, but it does not erase the need for leasing, rent growth, or recapitalization. There is still very little room for a sponsor to simply wait and hope the index bails out the capital stack.

That higher-for-longer base-rate backdrop is exactly why lending tone remains constructive but selective across channels. Banks are still open for top-tier relationship business, especially where there are deposits, moderate leverage, and clean property-level performance. But banks are not acting like yield tourists. They want lower leverage, tighter structures, and strong sponsorship. Life companies remain one of the cleanest sources of permanent capital for stabilized multifamily and industrial, especially where sponsors can live with moderate proceeds in exchange for certainty and a steadier spread conversation. CMBS is open, but it is open for deals that can clear with realistic underwriting, reserves, and structure. Debt funds continue to be essential for construction, lease-up, recapitalizations, and transitional assets, but flexibility is still expensive.

There are fresh signs that securitized markets remain functional on the front book. CREFC's April 28 securitized-debt update said five transactions totaling 3.6 billion dollars priced in the latest week. That included a 1.28 billion dollar industrial SASB and a 1.1 billion dollar Bridge Investment Group CRE CLO backed by a pool with a 90 percent minimum multifamily concentration. That is a useful read on execution tone. The market is open, senior pricing has behaved better than it did a year ago, but lower in the capital structure investors are still asking for real compensation and structure still matters.

At the same time, the back book remains stressed, and that split matters. CREFC's March 2026 CMBS loan performance report, also released April 28, showed overall delinquency rising to 7.55 percent and effective delinquency, which includes performing matured balloons, climbing to 9.07 percent. Office delinquency rose to 11.71 percent, and multifamily delinquency hit a cycle high of 7.15 percent. That is the whole picture in one sentence: fresh issuance can clear, but legacy loans are still running into a refinancing market that is much less forgiving than the one they were written into. So when people say spreads are better, that is true on the margin. It just does not mean the market is easy.

Multifamily remains the broadest live lending lane, and the last several days reinforced that. In New York, Commercial Observer reported on May 1 that Dwight Capital provided 114 million dollars of HUD 223(f) financing to refinance a newly built 168-unit apartment tower in Harlem. In Brooklyn, BridgeCity Capital provided 37.6 million dollars of acquisition and construction financing for a 71-unit mixed-use apartment project in Prospect Heights, also reported May 1. On the West Coast, Advanced Real Estate bought two Hollywood apartment towers in a 202 million dollar transaction backed by 141 million dollars of Freddie Mac financing. And in Charlotte, JLL arranged a 16 million dollar construction loan through Genesis Capital for an 84-unit project in South End.

Those are different deal types in different markets, but together they make the same point. Multifamily debt is still available across agency, HUD, construction, and bridge channels. What changes from deal to deal is not whether capital exists. It is the level of sponsorship quality, leverage discipline, and execution certainty required to get to a close.

The continuity story from the tracker also still holds. South Florida remains a live proving ground for apartment finance. Commercial Observer reported on April 30 that Dwight Capital closed a 130 million dollar HUD-backed refinance for Gardens Residences in North Miami. That deal followed the recent wave of Miami-area apartment financings that already suggested strong demand for good multifamily paper in growth markets. The point is not that lenders suddenly love every Sun Belt apartment story. The point is that when sponsorship is credible and the asset can support the debt, apartments still have more workable exits than most other property types.

Agency liquidity remains one of the strongest anchors in the sector. Fannie Mae reported on April 29 that first-quarter multifamily new business volume reached 17.1 billion dollars, the strongest first quarter in five years, financing about 110,000 units. The guaranty book grew to 542.5 billion dollars, though the serious delinquency rate ticked up to 0.78 percent from 0.74 percent at year-end. Freddie Mac said on April 30 that first-quarter multifamily new business activity reached 13 billion dollars, up 25 percent from a year earlier, with about 66 percent categorized as mission-driven affordable housing. Freddie also said its multifamily delinquency rate was 43 basis points at quarter-end, down from 44 basis points in the prior quarter and with 91 percent of the portfolio covered by credit enhancements.

That combination is important. Fannie is showing scale. Freddie is showing steady liquidity and credit support. Neither agency is pretending stress is gone, but both are still functioning as real execution channels at a time when many borrowers need certainty more than they need max proceeds. For stabilized or near-stabilized multifamily, that still makes agency debt the cleanest lane in much of the market.

HUD remains part of that story too. The HUD FHA Commercial Mortgage Portfolio page now shows February 2026 as the latest posted monthly portfolio report, which is not a breaking credit signal by itself, but the market evidence around it is clear. Borrowers continue to lean on HUD for long-duration fixed-rate takeouts where proceeds and amortization matter more than speed. That is why the Harlem and North Miami transactions matter. They are not one-off curiosities. They are proof that FHA-insured execution remains one of the few places where borrowers can still solve for duration and proceeds in a meaningful way if they can tolerate process.

If you step back, the spread story across lender groups is really a story about who gets paid to take what kind of risk. Banks can still be sharp on relationship pricing, but they want simplicity and balance-sheet value. Life companies can still offer attractive execution for durable income, but they are rarely the answer for a business-plan asset. CMBS can compete when sponsorship, structure, and in-place cash flow line up, especially for larger assets that fit securitization well. Debt funds remain the shock absorbers for everything messier than that, but they are charging for speed, flexibility, and uncertainty. So yes, spreads have normalized from the worst period, but they are normalizing into a market that still prices complexity aggressively.

Here is the markets snapshot heading into the new week. The latest official Treasury curve is still restrictive, with the 2-year at 3.88 percent, the 5-year at 4.02 percent, the 10-year at 4.39 percent, and the 30-year at 4.97 percent as of Friday, May 1. SOFR remains in the mid-3.6 percent range, so floating-rate carry is better than it was at the peak but still expensive in absolute terms. CMBS issuance remains open and top-of-stack spreads are a little tighter, but legacy delinquency is still high and multifamily distress in older loans continues to climb. Agency and HUD channels remain the clearest sources of apartment liquidity, while debt funds still own the complexity and construction lanes.

One thing to watch this week is whether macro volatility hardens lender posture just as fresh pipelines come in after month-end. If oil stays elevated, if the tariff fight with Europe escalates, or if the Treasury curve backs up again, lenders have every reason to stay disciplined on leverage and structure. But if rates simply hold here and multifamily deal flow keeps printing across agency, HUD, CMBS, and debt-fund channels, apartments may keep extending their lead as the most financeable large property type in the market.

The bottom line this morning is that the market still has capital, but not much slack. Nationally, the macro tone is being set by shipping risk in Hormuz, fresh Supreme Court and policy uncertainty, another immigration-funding fight, and a new tariff threat against Europe. In debt markets, that keeps money available but demanding. The encouraging part is that multifamily still has multiple real financing paths, from HUD to agency to selective bridge and construction capital. The harder truth is that elevated base rates are still doing most of the work. In this environment, the winners are still the same: credible sponsorship, realistic leverage, clean asset stories, and exits that a lender can underwrite without needing a heroic rate call.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 3: Hotter Inflation, a New Political Map Fight, and Multifamily Still Carrying the Tape
item.description: Today's Debt Desk opens with fresh national coverage of stronger first-quarter U.S. growth paired with hotter inflation, the Supreme Court's Voting Rights Act ruling and Florida's rapid redistricting follow-through, and the expanding tariff-refund story before moving into the latest Treasury curve and SOFR backdrop, current CRE lending tone across banks, life companies, CMBS and debt funds, fresh multifamily construction, bridge and HUD executions, agency activity from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Sunday, May 3, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

National News

The national story this morning still starts with a macro setup that is good enough to keep activity moving, but hot enough to keep borrowers and lenders uncomfortable. Thursday's official first-quarter data gave markets a cleaner look at that tension. Real GDP grew at a 2 percent annual rate in the first quarter after just 0.5 percent in the fourth quarter, so the economy clearly did not roll over. Investment, exports, consumer spending and government spending all added to growth. But the inflation buried inside that growth was the part the bond market could not ignore. The Bureau of Economic Analysis showed the quarterly PCE price index up 4.5 percent and core PCE up 4.3 percent. Its March personal income and outlays report added another reminder that household activity is still firm enough to keep pressure in the system, with income up 0.6 percent, spending up 0.9 percent and the monthly PCE price index up 0.7 percent. Then the Labor Department's Employment Cost Index came in at 0.9 percent for the quarter and 3.4 percent from a year earlier. Put that together and the message is pretty straightforward. The economy still has momentum, but inflation and labor costs are not cooling in a way that would make rates fall easily.

The second national story is political, but it matters for markets because it immediately touched the House map and the next fiscal fight. The Supreme Court's ruling that weakened a major Voting Rights Act challenge path was not left sitting in legal theory for long. Florida Republicans moved quickly on a new congressional map, and by Friday the coverage had shifted from what the ruling meant in principle to how fast states might use it in practice. That is why investors care. It brings the 2026 election map, the odds of House control changes, and the future path of taxes and spending negotiations a little closer to the front of the market conversation. The legal battles will continue, but the important shift is that redistricting is now an active operating story, not just a court headline.

The third national story is trade and corporate cash flow. The tariff-refund process is no longer an abstract post-Supreme Court cleanup job. It is turning into a live capital-allocation question for major companies. Reuters reported this week that automakers are banking on roughly $2.3 billion in future refunds, with General Motors alone expecting about $500 million back. What makes the story matter is not only the size of the refunds. It is what the process says about the next phase of trade policy. Companies are now making earnings calls, guidance decisions and working-capital plans around refunds, while they also try to model whatever replacement tariff structure the administration may pursue. So the trade story has moved out of pure Washington theory and into very practical balance-sheet territory.

That leaves the national backdrop in a fairly clear place for debt markets. Growth is still positive. Inflation is still sticky. Politics are introducing a live election-map fight with obvious fiscal consequences. And tariff litigation is feeding directly into corporate liquidity. None of that guarantees a shock this week, but all of it helps explain why rates are staying elevated and why lenders still feel no pressure to get aggressive.

Debt Desk

In rates, the latest official Treasury curve is still Friday, May 1, and it stayed high enough to keep the tone cautious. The 2-year closed at 3.88 percent, the 5-year at 4.02 percent, the 10-year at 4.39 percent and the 30-year at 4.97 percent. That matters because it is not just a 10-year story. The front end remains restrictive enough to hold floating-rate carry in place, while the long end is still high enough to limit how much permanent debt proceeds can really improve even when spreads behave. On the funding side, the latest published overnight SOFR remained 3.66 percent for April 30, and the 30-day average published on May 1 was 3.6485 percent. So the benchmark backdrop still says the same thing it said at the end of the week: borrowing costs are stable, but stable at a level that still forces discipline.

That is why the tone across lender groups still feels constructive but selective. The cleanest signal from the last few days came from the public-market intermediaries. JLL reported first-quarter results on April 30 showing broad-based capital-markets growth led by investment sales and debt advisory, with debt and equity advisory helping drive a 21 percent increase in capital markets services in local currency. Debt advisory revenue itself rose 30 percent year over year. Newmark's first-quarter results, also out April 30, carried a similar message: activity is improving and revenue is following it. Neither company is describing a wide-open market. But both are saying transaction pipelines and financing assignments are real, which is exactly what borrowers need to hear. The implication is that deals are getting done, but mostly when leverage is reasonable, sponsorship is experienced and the exit is visible.

For bank lenders, that usually means lower leverage, stronger cash flow and a clear story on recourse or reserves. Banks can still win on relationship pricing for the very best borrowers, especially in multifamily and industrial, but the hurdle is still high and regional exposure limits have not gone away. Life companies remain one of the cleaner permanent executions for stabilized assets, especially multifamily and industrial with durable income. They still like moderate leverage, and in this rate environment they can look attractive because spread execution is often steadier than the rest of the market even if absolute coupons are not cheap. Debt funds remain the venue for business-plan risk, construction, lease-up and complicated refinancings, but that flexibility still comes with a real price. The base rate is doing enough work on its own that a wide debt-fund spread can quickly push all-in cost into a range where the sponsor needs an unusually strong value-creation story.

CMBS remains the best example of a two-speed market. On the front book, the market is open. CREFC said five transactions totaling $3.6 billion priced in the latest week, including a $1.28 billion industrial SASB and a $1.1 billion Bridge-sponsored CRE CLO with a 90 percent minimum multifamily concentration. That is a useful reminder that securitized execution is there for the right collateral stack. But the legacy book is still stressed. CREFC's March 2026 loan performance report, released April 28, showed overall CMBS delinquency rising to 7.55 percent. Effective delinquency, which includes performing matured balloons, rose to 9.07 percent. Multifamily delinquency climbed to a cycle-high 7.15 percent, and office rose to 11.71 percent. So the split remains stark. New lending can clear. Old loans are still colliding with a refinancing market that is far less forgiving than the one they were originated into.

That split matters for spread talk. There are places where spreads have behaved better than they did a year ago, particularly for cleaner multifamily and stronger sponsors. But a tighter spread over a still-elevated Treasury or SOFR base does not automatically create relief. Plenty of borrowers are finding that execution feels better in relative terms while proceeds still feel constrained in absolute terms. In other words, the market has improved, but it has not become easy.

Multifamily remains the property type with the widest set of functioning debt channels, and last week's deal flow reinforced that point. S3 Capital provided a $344 million construction debt package for Continuum Company's waterfront development in Miami's North Bay Village, a sign that large-scale apartment construction can still secure debt-fund execution when the sponsorship and market story are strong enough. In Washington, D.C., NewBridge Lending provided a $50 million refinance for Clear Investment Group's 671-unit Langston Views through a new bridge-to-agency program. That deal is important because it shows there is still appetite for transitional apartment paper when the lender can see the agency takeout from the start. And in North Miami, Dwight Capital closed a $130 million HUD 223(f) refinance for Gardens Residences, described as the largest HUD loan ever executed in Florida. That one is a clean example of how HUD and FHA stay relevant when borrowers want long-duration, fully amortizing fixed-rate debt and can tolerate a slower process in exchange for certainty.

Those three deals also say something broader about multifamily execution today. Construction is open, but mostly through debt funds and only for very strong business plans. Transitional multifamily is financeable, especially when the bridge is explicitly designed to roll into agency debt. And stabilized or newly completed apartments with the right profile can still attract HUD or agency-backed permanent money. That is a much wider menu than most office, retail or hotel borrowers can claim right now.

Agency liquidity remains one of the clearest anchors in the apartment market. Fannie Mae's first-quarter multifamily update, released April 29, showed $17.1 billion in new multifamily business volume, its strongest first quarter in five years, financing roughly 110,000 units. The guaranty book grew to $542.5 billion, though the serious delinquency rate ticked up to 0.78 percent from 0.74 percent. Freddie Mac's first-quarter results, released April 30, said the company provided about $116 billion of total housing liquidity in the quarter, financing apartments as well as homes, and emphasized that most of the housing it supported was affordable to working families. The key takeaway is not that agencies are immune to stress. They are not. Delinquency pressure is rising there too. The takeaway is that they still represent one of the most reliable execution channels in the market, particularly for conventional and workforce multifamily.

That is why the multifamily story has to be told with both optimism and caution. The optimistic part is real. There is still active capital across agencies, HUD, bridge lenders, debt funds and CMBS. The cautious part is real too. Even in apartments, lenders are rewarding clean operations, realistic leverage and believable exits. And the distress data says not every multifamily loan written in the lower-rate era can refinance cleanly at today's coupons.

Here is the markets snapshot heading into the new week. The Treasury curve remains high across the front and long ends, with the 2-year at 3.88 percent, the 5-year at 4.02 percent, the 10-year at 4.39 percent and the 30-year at 4.97 percent as of Friday, May 1. Overnight SOFR held at 3.66 percent, while the 30-day average published Friday came in at 3.6485 percent. Public-market intermediaries are reporting better financing activity, which supports the idea that debt markets are functioning. But CMBS distress and rising multifamily delinquencies are still telling lenders to stay selective.

One thing to watch this week is whether the stronger growth and hotter inflation data start to harden lender posture even further just as borrowers come back in with fresh pipelines after month-end. If rates hold near current levels, banks, life companies, CMBS lenders and debt funds all have a reason to stay disciplined on leverage and structure. If the curve softens even modestly while apartment deal flow keeps printing, multifamily may widen its lead as the broadest live lending lane in the market.

The bottom line this morning is that the market still has movement, but it does not have much slack. Nationally, growth is better than feared, inflation is worse than hoped, and Washington keeps adding policy noise through redistricting and tariff spillovers. In debt markets, that keeps money available but demanding. The encouraging part is that multifamily still has multiple real execution paths, from bridge-to-agency to HUD to the GSEs to selective debt-fund construction capital. The harder truth is that spreads only matter so much when the base rate stays this high. For borrowers, the winning formula is still the same one it was last week: credible sponsorship, realistic leverage, clean asset-level storytelling and an exit that a lender can believe.

That is your Debt Desk for Sunday, May 3, 2026.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 2: Sticky Inflation, a Sharper Washington Fight, and Multifamily Capital That Still Shows Up
item.description: Today's Debt Desk opens with fresh national coverage of hotter first-quarter inflation inside stronger GDP, Kevin Warsh's advancing Fed transition, Florida's fast redistricting move after the Supreme Court's Voting Rights Act ruling, tariff refund fallout after GM quantified the stakes, and a risk-on stock tape before moving into the latest Treasury curve and SOFR prints, current CRE execution tone, fresh construction, bridge, and HUD multifamily deals, CMBS stress, agency activity, a concise ma
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Saturday, May 2, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

National News

The national story heading into the weekend still starts with the same uncomfortable macro combination, but Thursday's data made it harder to dismiss. The Bureau of Economic Analysis said first-quarter real GDP grew at a 2 percent annual rate after just 0.5 percent in the fourth quarter. On its own, that sounds like an economy that still has more underlying momentum than many borrowers and rate-watchers would prefer. Investment, exports, consumer spending, and government spending all helped. But the real issue for markets was the inflation embedded inside that growth. The same GDP release showed the PCE price index up 4.5 percent for the quarter and core PCE up 4.3 percent. Then BEA's March personal income and outlays report added another layer, with income up 0.6 percent, spending up 0.9 percent, and the monthly PCE price index up 0.7 percent. Add in the Employment Cost Index, which showed compensation up 0.9 percent in the first quarter and 3.4 percent over the year, and the message was clear. Growth is still alive, but inflation and wage pressure are not cooling cleanly enough to give the bond market much comfort.

The second national story is the Fed transition, which now feels less theoretical and more like a live calendar event. Kevin Warsh moved another step closer to the chair after the Senate Banking Committee approved him this week, and the larger Washington conversation has shifted from whether the White House wants a more aggressive turn at the Fed to how quickly that turn could happen and what kind of institution Warsh would inherit. The politics around it are unusually loud because Jerome Powell has said he plans to remain on the board for a period after his term as chair ends, which would be a break with modern practice and keeps the independence question front and center. For markets, that matters beyond headlines. The issue is not just who sits in the chair. It is whether rate policy, institutional credibility, and communication discipline all become more contested at the exact moment inflation is still running too warm for an easy pivot.

The third national thread is political control, and it sharpened this week after the Supreme Court narrowed a key Voting Rights Act pathway and Florida lawmakers moved almost immediately on a new congressional map. The speed is the point. When a court ruling changes the legal backdrop and a large state acts within days, investors do not treat it as abstract constitutional drama. They read it as an earlier and more structural fight over House control, tax policy, spending negotiations, and the odds of divided government. Florida's move is expected to face legal challenges, but the practical takeaway for markets is that the redistricting battle is now active, not hypothetical.

The fourth story is trade, where the legal unwind of the administration's prior tariff framework is turning into a current corporate cash-flow issue. General Motors said this week it expects a five hundred million dollar tariff refund after the Supreme Court struck down a large set of tariffs imposed under emergency powers. That does two things at once. It gives investors a concrete dollar figure for what the court's ruling means, and it reminds companies that the next phase of trade policy is not only about geopolitics. It is about balance-sheet timing, refund processing, supply-chain pricing, and how broad any replacement tariff structure might be. The U.S. Trade Representative's Section 301 hearing process is part of that reconstruction, but what matters right now is that the tariff story has already crossed from policy theory into earnings reality.

One more national point matters because it helps explain the split tone borrowers are seeing. Stocks finished Thursday with another strong record-setting session. The S and P 500 closed above 7,200, the Dow jumped nearly 800 points, and the Nasdaq also set a record. Equity investors were willing to celebrate strong earnings and steady growth even while the rates market kept staring at inflation. That divergence is important for real estate. Risk appetite can look healthy on the surface while debt capital remains expensive and selective underneath it.

That is the national backdrop going into the weekend. Now let us move into the Debt Desk.

Debt Desk

The first thing to know this morning is that the Treasury curve still is not giving borrowers much relief. The latest official U.S. Treasury par yield curve, published for Friday, May 1, showed the 2-year at 3.88 percent, the 5-year at 4.02 percent, the 10-year at 4.39 percent, and the 30-year at 4.97 percent. That profile matters because it keeps pressure on almost every part of the capital stack at once. The 2-year staying near 3.9 means floating-rate carry is still expensive. The 5-year above 4 percent keeps medium-duration permanent executions from feeling easy. The 10-year near 4.4 still crimps refinancing proceeds for plenty of conventional deals. And the 30-year just under 5 percent reminds everyone that long-duration money still wants compensation for inflation risk and policy uncertainty.

SOFR is telling essentially the same story. The latest published overnight print, reported Friday morning for Thursday, April 30 activity, was 3.66 percent, with the 30-day average at 3.64850 percent. That is stable enough to avoid fresh panic, but it is nowhere near low enough to rescue a weak bridge business plan. In the field, the benchmark still turns into an all-in coupon that feels heavy once spread, cap costs, reserve requirements, and structure get layered on. So the practical bridge-market reality remains familiar. Floating-rate debt is available, but it still demands either a very believable path to stabilization or a sponsor willing to pay for flexibility.

That helps explain why the execution tone across lender types still feels open but disciplined rather than loose. Fresh first-quarter commentary from the brokerage and advisory platforms says deal flow is improving. JLL reported that debt advisory revenue rose 30 percent year over year, and Newmark also pointed to stronger capital-markets momentum. That is meaningful because it shows transactions are happening again. But it does not mean every lender has turned generous. Banks still want relationship value, clean basis, and conservative leverage. Life companies are showing up for high-quality multifamily and industrial but are not stretching for problem structure. CMBS remains viable for scaled, cleaner stories that can handle securitized execution. Debt funds still own the complexity trade, especially construction, repositioning, and bridge-to-agency, but they are pricing that flexibility accordingly.

The recent deal tape is a good read on where real conviction still exists. In Miami, S3 Capital provided a $344 million construction financing package for Continuum Company's waterfront development in North Bay Village. That is a large loan with real risk attached to it, and the takeaway is not that construction money is broadly easy. It is that private credit will still write size when the location is elite, the sponsor is experienced, and the exit story is strong enough to overcome today's base-rate backdrop. It is targeted aggression, not market-wide looseness.

In Washington, D.C., Clear Investment Group secured a $50 million refinancing from NewBridge Lending for Langston Views through a bridge-to-agency structure. That deal says a lot in a small number. It shows that transitional multifamily can still find capital when the sponsor has already done meaningful operational cleanup and when the lender can underwrite not just today's property but the next takeout. Bridge-to-agency remains one of the more credible lanes in the market because it offers a visible exit path, and visible exits matter more when rates are high and capital is choosy.

Then there is the HUD and FHA side, which again showed why it remains such an important outlet in this market. Dwight Capital closed a $130 million HUD 223(f) refinance for Gardens Residences in North Miami. That matters beyond the one property. When conventional permanent debt feels expensive and debt funds are charging for complexity, FHA-backed execution can still provide a useful answer for stabilized apartment product that can tolerate the timeline. In other words, process is still the price of certainty in part of the multifamily market.

Agency liquidity also continues to separate itself from the rest of the field. Fannie Mae said its first-quarter multifamily new business volume reached $17.1 billion, the strongest first quarter in five years, with its guaranty book growing to $542.5 billion. Freddie Mac followed with first-quarter multifamily new business activity of $13 billion, up 25 percent from a year earlier, while its multifamily portfolio reached $498 billion and its delinquency rate edged down to 43 basis points. Those are not just institutional scorecards. They are evidence that the agencies remain among the deepest and most dependable capital channels for apartments at a moment when many conventional lenders still want every underwrite to look nearly bulletproof.

Freddie also offered a useful execution signal this week by highlighting changes to its full-term interest-only structures to align minimum debt-coverage requirements with amortizing and partial interest-only offerings, with the goal of reducing quote times. That is not a loosening story so much as an efficiency story. But in this market, efficiency matters. Borrowers and intermediaries care about certainty of execution almost as much as spread because missed timing can destroy a refinancing just as effectively as a wide coupon.

The CMBS market continues to send a mixed message, especially for multifamily. On the front book, issuance and lending are still functioning. CREFC said five transactions totaling $3.6 billion priced in the latest week, including a $1.28 billion industrial SASB and a $1.1 billion Bridge-sponsored CRE CLO with a 90 percent minimum multifamily concentration. That tells you securitized markets are still very much open for the right collateral and sponsorship. But the back book is still under real pressure. CREFC's March 2026 loan performance report showed overall CMBS delinquency at 7.55 percent and effective delinquency, including performing matured balloons, at 9.07 percent.
