Multifamily delinquency rose to 7.15 percent, a cycle high, while office delinquency reached 11.71 percent. That is the split in one frame: new paper can clear, but old underwriting problems are still very much alive.

That is why spread talk needs to stay nuanced. Earlier April research from CRED iQ showed permanent loan spreads over Treasurys had tightened year over year, especially in multifamily, which is one reason more refinancings are penciling today than they did a year ago. But tighter spread over a still-elevated base rate only gets you so far. Borrowers may be winning relative value and still losing absolute proceeds. For lenders, that means the market can feel healthier and still remain structurally conservative.

Multifamily continues to stand out because it has several functioning capital lanes at once. Agency execution is deep. HUD remains relevant. Bridge-to-agency is working for transitional deals with a credible cleanup story. Debt funds are still funding construction and complex repositionings where the basis and sponsorship hold up. CMBS is still available for larger institutional stories. That does not mean apartment lending is easy. It means multifamily remains the broadest live lending lane in a market where many other property types still have fewer answers.

Here is the markets snapshot heading into next week. The latest official Treasury curve sits at 3.88 percent on the 2-year, 4.02 percent on the 5-year, 4.39 percent on the 10-year, and 4.97 percent on the 30-year. The latest published overnight SOFR is 3.66 percent, with the 30-day average at 3.64850 percent. Agency liquidity still looks like the cleanest multifamily channel. Fresh deal flow says construction, bridge-to-agency, and HUD-backed apartment executions are all still getting done, but only where the story is unusually clear. CMBS remains a two-speed market, with functioning new issuance and a still-troubled legacy book.

One thing to watch next week is whether the hotter first-quarter inflation data starts to feed through into even tighter lender posture before the next round of macro releases and Fed-transition headlines. If Treasury yields stay pinned near current levels and Washington keeps adding policy noise, lenders have little reason to loosen on leverage or spread. If the curve drifts lower and execution pipelines keep building, multifamily borrowers may get a slightly better window than the broader CRE market.

The bottom line this morning is that the market still has motion, but not simplicity. The economy is growing faster than it did at the end of last year, yet inflation is still sticky enough to keep rates restrictive. Washington is adding a live Fed transition, a sharper redistricting fight, and ongoing trade-policy uncertainty to the mix. In debt markets, that means the capital stack is open, but it continues to reward precision over optimism. The encouraging part is that multifamily still has real options across agencies, HUD, bridge, debt funds, and securitized channels. The harder truth is that borrowers still need clean stories, realistic leverage, and credible exits, because money is available, but it is not forgiving.

That is your Debt Desk for Saturday, May 2, 2026.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for May 1: Hotter Inflation, a Live Fed Handoff, and CRE Credit That Still Wants Clarity
item.description: Today's Debt Desk opens with fresh national coverage of stronger first-quarter growth, hotter inflation, the next phase of Kevin Warsh's path to the Fed chair, the Voting Rights Act ruling and Florida remap fallout, and the administration's tariff rebuild before moving into the latest official Treasury curve, SOFR, current CRE execution tone, fresh multifamily financings, CMBS stress, agency activity, a concise markets snapshot, and one thing to watch next.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Friday, May 1, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

National News

The national setup this morning starts with a familiar but uncomfortable combination: the economy still has forward motion, but inflation is not cooperating enough to make life easy for the rate market. On Thursday, April 30, the Bureau of Economic Analysis said first-quarter real GDP grew at a 2 percent annual rate after a soft 0.5 percent pace in the fourth quarter. That sounds like decent momentum, and in one sense it is. Business investment held up, exports improved, and government spending also contributed. But the same release also showed the PCE price index running at 4.5 percent for the quarter, with core PCE at 4.3 percent. That is the part the bond market cannot ignore. The economy is not falling apart fast enough to force relief, and prices are still running hot enough to keep the Fed boxed in.

That inflation pressure looked even more real when the rest of Thursday's data hit. BEA said personal income rose 0.6 percent in March and spending rose 0.9 percent, while the Employment Cost Index from the Bureau of Labor Statistics showed compensation costs up 0.9 percent in the first quarter and 3.4 percent over the last year. Put all of that together and the story is pretty straightforward. Demand is not dead, wage pressure is not gone, and inflation has not cooled enough to give policymakers or lenders much confidence that easier money is around the corner. This is the same rates story from yesterday, but now it has fresh economic data behind it.

That rolls directly into the second big national story, which is still the Fed handoff. The market already had Wednesday's rate hold and the unusually split vote to process. Now it also has a clearer political timetable. After the Senate Banking Committee advanced Kevin Warsh on Wednesday, Senate leadership filed cloture on Thursday, April 30, setting up the next step in his path to confirmation when the chamber returns on Monday, May 11. So the story is no longer just that Jerome Powell appears to be near the end of his term as chair. The story is that investors now have an actual calendar for the transition, and they are trying to handicap what a Warsh-led Fed would mean for credibility, communication, and the White House's very public pressure for lower rates.

There is also a third Washington story that keeps gaining weight because it could reshape the political map faster than investors expected. The Supreme Court's ruling this week narrowing a key Voting Rights Act pathway was immediately followed by Florida lawmakers approving a new congressional map. The practical market takeaway is not about any single district. It is that the fight for House control has become more structural and more front-loaded. When the legal rules around redistricting change and states move quickly, markets start repricing the odds of divided government, tax fights, and fiscal negotiations earlier than they otherwise would.

The fourth national thread is trade policy, and it still deserves attention because it has moved from legal cleanup into active reconstruction. The U.S. Trade Representative wrapped Section 301 hearings this week after holding them on April 28 and April 29, and the administration is still trying to build a replacement framework after the Supreme Court struck down its earlier emergency-powers tariff approach. That process matters because companies now have to underwrite not just whether tariffs come back, but how broad, durable, and administratively workable the next version might be. General Motors already gave the market a concrete reminder of the stakes when it said this week that it expects a five hundred million dollar refund tied to the invalidated tariffs. So trade is not just a politics story. It is already an earnings, liquidity, and pricing story.

One more piece of context before we leave the national section. Equity markets ended April with a risk-on tone even as the rates backdrop stayed difficult. Associated Press reported that on Thursday, April 30, the S and P 500 closed at a fresh record, the Dow posted a strong gain, and the Nasdaq also hit a record. In other words, stocks were willing to celebrate earnings strength while bonds kept staring at inflation. That split matters for commercial real estate because owners and lenders are still operating in a market where headline risk appetite can look healthy, but debt capital remains disciplined underneath it.

That is the national picture this morning. Now let us move into the Debt Desk.

Debt Desk

The most important thing in debt markets this morning is still the rates backdrop, and the latest official Treasury curve is not giving borrowers much relief. The U.S. Treasury's April 30 par yield curve showed the two-year at 3.88 percent, the five-year at 4.02 percent, the ten-year at 4.40 percent, and the thirty-year at 4.98 percent. That is only a modest improvement from the prior day's post-Fed backup, but the larger message is unchanged. The front end is still high enough to keep floating-rate carry expensive, the five-year is still high enough to pressure medium-duration permanent executions, the ten-year is still a proceeds problem for plenty of conventional refinancings, and the long bond is still telling you the market wants compensation for inflation and policy uncertainty.

SOFR is reinforcing that same message. The latest official New York Fed print, published Friday morning for Thursday, April 29 activity, put SOFR at 3.63 percent, with the 30-day average at 3.64917 percent. That is not a shocking number by itself, but in CRE it still lands hard once lender spread, reserve requirements, cap costs, and structure all get added back in. A benchmark in the low 3.6s can still become an all-in coupon that feels punishing for a transitional asset. So the practical reality for bridge borrowers remains the same. The base rate is no longer exploding higher, but it is also nowhere near low enough to bail out a weak business plan.

That is why execution still feels open but selective. Fresh signals from the brokerage and advisory platforms back that up. JLL said on Thursday that its Capital Markets Services business was up 21 percent in the first quarter, and that debt advisory revenue grew 30 percent year over year across nearly all sectors. Commercial Observer also reported that Newmark posted better-than-expected first-quarter revenue and raised full-year guidance. Neither of those data points means credit is loose. What they do mean is that transaction professionals are finding business again, because lenders are showing up for the right assets and the right sponsors. The market is functioning. It is just functioning with discipline.

The recent deal tape makes that pretty clear. On the debt-fund and construction side, Commercial Observer reported Thursday that S3 Capital provided a 344 million dollar construction package for Continuum Company's waterfront development in North Bay Village, Miami. That is a large, conviction-heavy deal, and it tells you private credit will still write size when the location, sponsorship, and sell-through story are strong enough. It is not broad-based generosity. It is targeted risk-taking.

On the bridge side, Commercial Observer also reported Thursday that Clear Investment Group secured a 50 million dollar refinancing for Langston Views, a 671-unit property in southeast Washington, D.C., from NewBridge Lending through a bridge-to-agency structure. That is a useful read-through because it shows one of the more active lanes in this market. Transitional multifamily can still get financed when the lender has a believable path to an agency exit and the sponsor has already started the operational cleanup. It also tells you that bridge lenders are not just underwriting today's cash flow. They are underwriting the next capital source.

And on the HUD and agency-linked side, Thursday brought another clean multifamily example. Commercial Observer reported that Dwight Capital closed a 130 million dollar HUD 223(f) loan to refinance the Gardens Residences in North Miami. That deal matters because it reinforces something borrowers already know but sometimes forget during volatile rate weeks. When conventional capital is expensive and fast money is selective, FHA execution still has real value for stabilized apartment product that can tolerate the process.

Multifamily remains one of the few areas where several capital channels are still clearly alive at the same time, and the agencies underscored that again this week. Fannie Mae said on April 29 that first-quarter multifamily new business volume reached 17.1 billion dollars, its strongest first quarter in five years, and that its guaranty book grew to 542.5 billion dollars. Freddie Mac followed with its own first-quarter update on April 30, saying multifamily new business activity hit 13 billion dollars, up 25 percent from a year earlier, while its multifamily portfolio reached 498 billion dollars and delinquency held at 43 basis points. That is not just balance-sheet trivia. It is confirmation that agency liquidity remains one of the cleanest and deepest lanes in the apartment market.

Freddie also gave the market a smaller but useful operational signal this week. In a multifamily underwriting update published April 30, the company said it adjusted certain full-term interest-only structures to align minimum debt-coverage requirements with its amortizing and partial interest-only offerings, with the aim of reducing quote times for lenders. That does not mean terms suddenly got easy. It means the agencies are still working on execution certainty, and in this market certainty itself has value.

The CMBS side continues to tell a split story, especially for multifamily. The front book is still open for quality collateral and scaled sponsorship, but the legacy book keeps reminding everyone how little room there is for old underwriting assumptions. The latest CREFC March loan performance report, released April 28 and still the freshest broad industry read at the end of this week, showed overall CMBS delinquency at 7.55 percent and effective delinquency including performing matured balloons at 9.07 percent. Multifamily delinquency rose to 7.15 percent, a cycle high, while office delinquency pushed to 11.71 percent. That is the picture lenders are underwriting against. New deals can get done, but nobody is ignoring the stress sitting behind them.

That is also why spread talk needs nuance. Earlier April research from CRED iQ showed permanent-loan spreads over Treasurys had compressed from a year ago, especially in multifamily, which helps explain why some refinancings are possible now that were impossible a year earlier. But compression in spread does not automatically mean easy proceeds, because the underlying Treasury curve is still elevated. Borrowers may be paying a tighter spread over a higher base rate, which is better than last year in relative terms and still painful in absolute terms. In plain English, debt is available, but math still rules.

Across lender types, the tone still breaks down the same way. Banks are active where there is relationship value, good basis, and low leverage. Life-company capital is still credible for clean multifamily and industrial, but it is not chasing stretch structure. CMBS is viable for larger, cleaner stories that can stand up in securitized execution. Debt funds still own the complexity trade, especially for construction, repositioning, and bridge-to-agency strategies, but they are charging for flexibility because they know they can.

Here is the concise markets snapshot.

The latest official Treasury curve from April 30 sits at 3.88 percent on the two-year, 4.02 percent on the five-year, 4.40 percent on the ten-year, and 4.98 percent on the thirty-year.

The latest official SOFR print is 3.63 percent, with the 30-day average at 3.64917 percent.

Agency liquidity still looks like the cleanest lane in multifamily, with Fannie and Freddie both posting solid first-quarter activity and credit metrics that remain manageable.

Fresh deal flow says construction, bridge-to-agency, and HUD-backed apartment executions are all still happening, but only where the story is unusually clear.

CMBS remains a two-speed market, with new issuance and new lending functioning while legacy maturities and delinquencies continue to drag through the back book.

One thing to watch next is whether Friday's labor data changes the rate conversation before the market gets to the next Warsh confirmation milestone on Monday, May 11. If jobs stay firm and inflation pressure still feels sticky, lenders have no reason to get looser on spread or leverage. If labor softens enough to pull the curve down meaningfully, borrowers may get a narrower and more useful execution window than they had earlier this week.

The bottom line this morning is that the macro story did not simplify. Thursday gave the market stronger growth, hotter inflation, steady consumer spending, firm wage pressure, a live calendar for the Fed leadership transition, and no real sign that trade policy is becoming less consequential. In debt markets, that translated into a Treasury curve that is still high, a SOFR backdrop that still makes floating-rate debt expensive, and a lending market that still rewards clarity more than optimism. The good news is that multifamily remains financeable through several channels at once, and real deals are still getting done. The harder truth is that borrowers still need clean stories, realistic leverage, and credible exits, because the market is open, but it is not forgiving.

That is your Debt Desk for Friday, May 1, 2026.
